title: Nasogastric Tube Insertion
oneLiner: Insertion of a tube from nose to stomach for feeding/decompression; failure to confirm position is the most common cause of fatal error.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pleural placement (tube in bronchus/pleura):** sudden respiratory distress, hypoxia, inability to aspirate, pH > 6, CXR shows tube deviating into lung → stop feeding, remove tube, get urgent CXR, call senior.
- **Oesophageal perforation:** chest pain, dysphagia, subcutaneous emphysema, shock → stop insertion, CXR (check for mediastinal air), urgent ENT/surgical referral, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** patient requires gastric decompression (ileus, obstruction), feeding (dysphagia, frailty), or medication delivery; often nil by mouth until tube inserted.
- **Key findings on insertion:** inability to pass beyond pharynx, patient coughing/gagging, resistance at oropharynx; after placement – aspiration of gastric fluid (green/brown, pH < 5.5) confirms correct position.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Malposition (bronchial/pleural)** → pH > 6, cannot aspirate, respiratory symptoms, CXR shows tube in airway.
- **Coiled in oesophagus** → aspirate pH > 6 (saliva), patient may be able to speak / cough, CXR shows tube tip above diaphragm.
- **Correct gastric placement** → aspirate pH < 5.5, routine CXR confirms tip below left hemidiaphragm.
- **Splitter:** pH of gastric aspirate ≤ 5.5 strongly supports gastric placement; if pH > 5.5, must have CXR before any use.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** pH testing of aspirate (use pH‑specific paper, not litmus); measure tube length (3 marks – NEX); check for respiratory distress or cough.
- **Bloods:** none routinely required for insertion; if patient on anticoagulants, check INR (risk of epistaxis).
- **Imaging:** CXR (AP) – gold standard if pH is borderline or aspirate not obtained; look for tube tip below diaphragm and no deviation.
- **Special tests:** capnography (if available) – end‑tidal CO₂ suggests tracheal placement.
- **Never‑miss test:** CXR before first feed or medication if pH > 5.5 or no aspirate obtained – omission risks fatal pleural feeding.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** position patient upright (if safe), apply lubrication, measure NEX distance, insert with gentle pressure (ask patient to swallow if conscious). Confirm position – pH test, then CXR if needed.
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):** secure tube with tape, document external length and date. Before each feed/medication, re‑check pH and tube length. Use a closed feeding system if possible.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** replace tube every 6‑8 weeks (or per local policy); monitor for tube blockage, sinusitis, or skin irritation at nares.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** nil by mouth until position confirmed. Start feed at [check local policy] rate if for nutrition. VTE prophylaxis if immobile. Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:** none for insertion itself. For difficult insertion due to gastric stasis → metoclopramide 10 mg IV (single dose, caution in young patients – risk of dystonic reaction). For nasal mucosal vasoconstriction → xylometazoline 0.1% nasal spray, 1 spray in each nostril (max 2 sprays per nostril per day).
- **Local anaesthetic:** lidocaine 1% spray (max 5 mg/kg) to pharynx if patient anxious/gagging [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications:** severe coagulopathy (hold if INR > 3), facial fractures (risk of intracranial placement – use oral route instead), recent oesophageal surgery.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** using blue litmus paper instead of pH‑graded paper – false colour change can lead to feeding into the lung. Always use pH paper with range 0–6.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** pleural placement (pneumothorax, empyema), oesophageal perforation, aspiration pneumonia, epistaxis, vasovagal syncope, tube coiling.
- **Chronic:** tube blockage, tube fracture (retained fragment risk), sinusitis (prolonged NG use), nasal septal erosion.
sections.Prognosis: - Most insertions are safe and well tolerated if position is confirmed. Undetected pleural placement has mortality > 50% within hours if feeding is started.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always** test pH of the **first** aspirate – if pH < 5.5, you can proceed safely. If pH > 5.5 or no aspirate, **do not feed** – organise CXR.
- Use a two‑person check for tube length and pH if you are busy – reduces error.
- Document the external tube length at the nostril every shift – a sudden increase suggests tube has migrated.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never** rely on “air insufflation + auscultation” to confirm position – it is unreliable and has caused deaths. 
- **Do not** use a tube that has been flushed with water before pH testing – water will dilute aspirate and give a falsely high pH.
- **Do not** re‑attempt insertion in a patient with known oesophageal varices without senior approval.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, or vomiting within 30 minutes of a feed; tube has moved (marker longer at nostril); feed comes out of mouth/nose.
- **Follow-up:** community nursing team for daily tube care and re‑check pH if any concerns.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:** pH > 6 and CXR shows tube tip above diaphragm or in a bronchus; patient develops acute respiratory distress during insertion (suspect pneumothorax); failure to insert after 2 attempts (or 1 in a sedated/unsafe patient).
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, SpO₂ < 92% on air, new‑onset chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema.

rawMarkdown:
## Nasogastric Tube Insertion
**One-liner:** Insertion of a tube from nose to stomach for feeding/decompression; failure to confirm position is the most common cause of fatal error.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pleural placement (tube in bronchus/pleura):** sudden respiratory distress, hypoxia, inability to aspirate, pH > 6, CXR shows tube deviating into lung → stop feeding, remove tube, get urgent CXR, call senior.
- **Oesophageal perforation:** chest pain, dysphagia, subcutaneous emphysema, shock → stop insertion, CXR (check for mediastinal air), urgent ENT/surgical referral, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** patient requires gastric decompression (ileus, obstruction), feeding (dysphagia, frailty), or medication delivery; often nil by mouth until tube inserted.
- **Key findings on insertion:** inability to pass beyond pharynx, patient coughing/gagging, resistance at oropharynx; after placement – aspiration of gastric fluid (green/brown, pH < 5.5) confirms correct position.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Malposition (bronchial/pleural)** → pH > 6, cannot aspirate, respiratory symptoms, CXR shows tube in airway.
- **Coiled in oesophagus** → aspirate pH > 6 (saliva), patient may be able to speak / cough, CXR shows tube tip above diaphragm.
- **Correct gastric placement** → aspirate pH < 5.5, routine CXR confirms tip below left hemidiaphragm.
- **Splitter:** pH of gastric aspirate ≤ 5.5 strongly supports gastric placement; if pH > 5.5, must have CXR before any use.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** pH testing of aspirate (use pH‑specific paper, not litmus); measure tube length (3 marks – NEX); check for respiratory distress or cough.
- **Bloods:** none routinely required for insertion; if patient on anticoagulants, check INR (risk of epistaxis).
- **Imaging:** CXR (AP) – gold standard if pH is borderline or aspirate not obtained; look for tube tip below diaphragm and no deviation.
- **Special tests:** capnography (if available) – end‑tidal CO₂ suggests tracheal placement.
- **Never‑miss test:** CXR before first feed or medication if pH > 5.5 or no aspirate obtained – omission risks fatal pleural feeding.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** position patient upright (if safe), apply lubrication, measure NEX distance, insert with gentle pressure (ask patient to swallow if conscious). Confirm position – pH test, then CXR if needed.
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):** secure tube with tape, document external length and date. Before each feed/medication, re‑check pH and tube length. Use a closed feeding system if possible.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** replace tube every 6‑8 weeks (or per local policy); monitor for tube blockage, sinusitis, or skin irritation at nares.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** nil by mouth until position confirmed. Start feed at [check local policy] rate if for nutrition. VTE prophylaxis if immobile. Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:** none for insertion itself. For difficult insertion due to gastric stasis → metoclopramide 10 mg IV (single dose, caution in young patients – risk of dystonic reaction). For nasal mucosal vasoconstriction → xylometazoline 0.1% nasal spray, 1 spray in each nostril (max 2 sprays per nostril per day).
- **Local anaesthetic:** lidocaine 1% spray (max 5 mg/kg) to pharynx if patient anxious/gagging [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications:** severe coagulopathy (hold if INR > 3), facial fractures (risk of intracranial placement – use oral route instead), recent oesophageal surgery.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** using blue litmus paper instead of pH‑graded paper – false colour change can lead to feeding into the lung. Always use pH paper with range 0–6.

### Complications
- **Acute:** pleural placement (pneumothorax, empyema), oesophageal perforation, aspiration pneumonia, epistaxis, vasovagal syncope, tube coiling.
- **Chronic:** tube blockage, tube fracture (retained fragment risk), sinusitis (prolonged NG use), nasal septal erosion.

### Prognosis
- Most insertions are safe and well tolerated if position is confirmed. Undetected pleural placement has mortality > 50% within hours if feeding is started.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always** test pH of the **first** aspirate – if pH < 5.5, you can proceed safely. If pH > 5.5 or no aspirate, **do not feed** – organise CXR.
- Use a two‑person check for tube length and pH if you are busy – reduces error.
- Document the external tube length at the nostril every shift – a sudden increase suggests tube has migrated.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never** rely on “air insufflation + auscultation” to confirm position – it is unreliable and has caused deaths. 
- **Do not** use a tube that has been flushed with water before pH testing – water will dilute aspirate and give a falsely high pH.
- **Do not** re‑attempt insertion in a patient with known oesophageal varices without senior approval.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, or vomiting within 30 minutes of a feed; tube has moved (marker longer at nostril); feed comes out of mouth/nose.
- **Follow-up:** community nursing team for daily tube care and re‑check pH if any concerns.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:** pH > 6 and CXR shows tube tip above diaphragm or in a bronchus; patient develops acute respiratory distress during insertion (suspect pneumothorax); failure to insert after 2 attempts (or 1 in a sedated/unsafe patient).
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, SpO₂ < 92% on air, new‑onset chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema.
